title: Chapter 10 — Capstone: One Project, Three Claude Surfaces
CHAPTER 10 —
CAPSTONE: ONE PROJECT, THREE CLAUDE SURFACES
segments.body_html: The capstone routes one real project through the correct Claude surfaces, with explicit routing decisions, approval gate

beats.text: The capstone routes one real project through the correct Claude surfaces, with explicit routing decisions, approval gates, verification evidence, and a reflection that transfers to the next project. The deliverable is not just the final artifact , it is the process record that shows your judgment at each step. Claude can draft, assemble, run scripts, and organize files. The claim, the evidence, the permission, and the final action stay human-owned. This chapter ends the book by asking you to do the full loop once, on work that matters to you.

segments.label: 01 / 13 · HOOK

title: OPENING SCENE
segments.body_html:
Here is a project that happens in real life: a researcher needs a briefing note summarizing recent literature on a speci

Opening Scene — Without Claude, the researcher searches databases, reads abstracts, builds the matrix by hand, drafts the note, edits it

beats.text: Here is a project that happens in real life: a researcher needs a briefing note summarizing recent literature on a specialized topic, formatted for a non-specialist audience, with a source matrix appended. It is about four hours of focused work. Not a dissertation, not a quick search , something in between. Without Claude, the researcher searches databases, reads abstracts, builds the matrix by hand, drafts the note, edits it twice, and sends it. With Claude, the same project runs differently. But how?

segments.label: 02 / 13 · OPENING SCENE

segments.section: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO
title: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS
YOU DO.
segments.body_html:
·Decompose a real project into tasks and route each task to the correct Claude surface
·Set up a project workspace, write task packet specifications, and run approval gates
·Collect verification evidence at each hand-off
·Produce an AI-use log and a reflection that transfers to your next project
·Recognize when to use all three surfaces and when to use fewer

beats.text: By the end of this chapter you will be able to: Decompose a real project into tasks and route each task to the correct Claude surface Set up a project workspace, write task packet specifications, and run approval gates Collect verification evidence at each hand-off Produce an AI-use log and a reflection that transfers to your next project Recognize when to use all three surfaces and when to use fewer

segments.label: 03 / 13 · WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

title: BEFORE YOU BEGIN:
PROJECT SELECTION
segments.body_html:
·Small enough to complete in a few hours
·Rich enough to need at least two Claude surfaces
Real enough that the output mattersa document you will actually use, share, or submit
·Safe enough that you are not routing genuinely sensitive, proprietary, or human-subjects data through AI systems under t
·A briefing note on a topic in your field for a non-specialist audience

beats.text: The capstone works best on a project that is: Small enough to complete in a few hours Rich enough to need at least two Claude surfaces Real enough that the output matters , a document you will actually use, share, or submit Safe enough that you are not routing genuinely sensitive, proprietary, or human-subjects data through AI systems under time pressure

segments.label: 04 / 13 · BEFORE YOU BEGIN: PROJECT SELE

title: THE WORKED EXAMPLE:
A RESEARCH BRIEFING NOTE
segments.body_html:
·What should Claude produce?
·What context does Claude need?
·What are the constraints?
·What will I verify before using the output?
·Does the citation match a real document in your folder?

beats.text: The worked example is a research briefing note. The project: summarize recent evidence on the use of AI tools in higher education advising, for an audience of non-specialist administrators, with a source matrix appended. Approximately four pages plus a two-column source table. Walk through this example in detail. Then apply the same structure to your chosen project.

segments.label: 05 / 13 · THE WORKED EXAMPLE: A RESEARCH

title: THE CAPSTONE
ARTIFACT BUNDLE.
segments.body_html:
Project briefone paragraph, including the data/privacy boundary note
Surface-routing mapone table, with rationale for each routing decision
Task packet specificationsone packet per AI-assisted task
AI-use logone table, with gate checks and actions taken
Verification checklistthe human-gate checks you ran (sources, calculations, claims, figures)

beats.text: Your capstone is complete when you have produced: Project brief , one paragraph, including the data/privacy boundary note Surface-routing map , one table, with rationale for each routing decision Task packet specifications , one packet per AI-assisted task AI-use log , one table, with gate checks and actions taken Verification checklist , the human-gate checks you ran (sources, calculations, claims, figures) Final artifact , the actual deliverable (the briefing note, the module outline, the data story, the process document) Reflection note , six structured questions plus two transfer

segments.label: 06 / 13 · THE CAPSTONE ARTIFACT BUNDLE

title: CONSTRAINED VERSION:
CHAT-ONLY SIMULATION
segments.body_html:
·Replace Cowork file assembly with manual copy-paste from verified sources into a structured table you build yourself
·Replace Claude Code scripts with manual spreadsheet work or find-and-replace
·Keep all four gates; they apply in chat-only workflows too
·The project brief and boundary note
·The task packet specification for each chat session

beats.text: If you do not have access to Claude Cowork or Claude Code, run the same project using Claude AI (chat) only and manual file work. What changes: Replace Cowork file assembly with manual copy-paste from verified sources into a structured table you build yourself Replace Claude Code scripts with manual spreadsheet work or find-and-replace Keep all four gates; they apply in chat-only workflows too

segments.label: 07 / 13 · CONSTRAINED VERSION: CHAT-ONLY

title: COMMON MISTAKES
segments.body_html: Treating the final artifact as the capstone deliverable.→Using all three surfaces because the chapter says to.→Letting Cowork or Code run to completion before reviewing intermediate outputs.→Asking Claude to verify its own work.→Writing a reflection that describes what happened without transferring to future practice.

beats.text: Treating the final artifact as the capstone deliverable. A polished briefing note or slide deck is not evidence of a trustworthy workflow. It is the output of one. The process artifacts , routing map, task packets, log, verification checklist, reflection , are what show that the workflow was governed. Using all three surfaces because the chapter says to. If your project does not need Claude Code, do not use Claude Code. Unnecessary surface use adds complexity and risk without adding value.

segments.label: 08 / 13 · COMMON MISTAKES

beats.text: You opened this book at a polished artifact that might not be trustworthy. Chapters 1 through 9 gave you a set of tools for telling the difference: task diagnosis, surface selection, specification, context design, privacy discipline, verification, and workflow design. This chapter asked you to use all of them, once, on something real.
